teamObjective.explanation: As the opening leader with a very weak hand (3-6 of spades, no trumps), the immediate objective is to lead a low-card trick cheaply and retain whatever small card advantage remains for later tricks, while observing how opponents and partner respond.

partnerBelief.summary: Partner (player 2) holds 3 unknown cards. At game start there are no public signals about suit distribution or strength; partner could be strong or weak. I cannot infer any specific holdings.

opponentBelief.summary: Opponents (players 1 and 3) each hold 3 unknown cards. With no cards played, their hands are uniformly uncertain; either may have trumps, higher spades, or other suits. I must assume they can likely beat any low spade lead.

actionRationale.primaryReason: Leading the 3 of spades expends the weakest card first. All legal options are low non-trump singles, so the opening lead should minimize future liability by keeping the slightly higher 4-6 for later tricks where they might win or force opponents to use trumps.

whyNotAlternatives.reason: Playing the highest spade now wastes the only card that might win a later low-rank trick, with no compensating benefit on an opening lead.
The 4 is still marginally stronger than the 3; leading it leaves the 3 as a later liability without increasing the chance of winning this trick.
The 5 is the second-strongest card in hand; preserving it for a later trick is better than using it on an opening lead that opponents can easily overtake.

risks: Any opponent may have a higher spade or trump and take the lead immediately.
Partner may be unable to support this trick, but that is unknown at game start.
The hand is weak overall, so losing this trick is likely.
riskAssessment.mitigation: By leading the lowest card, I minimize information given away and keep slightly better cards for future tricks; I can observe the response and adjust subsequent play.

notes: Opening lead with four consecutive low spades and no trumps; no partner or opponent signals exist yet.
